Results, Discussion, and Conclusion

Results Figures and Tables Discussion Conclusion
Winning LLM
GPT-5.2-Pro

83 vote points, 45% of all points awarded across both batches.

Winning Blend
Blend C

62 vote points; highest T0 pleasantness, T15 pleasantness, and harmony.

Metric Agreement
rho = 0.837

Vote points and weighted performance score ranked blends in strong agreement (p = 0.001).

Strongest Chemistry Link
Base notes

Base note percentage correlated most strongly with vote points (rho = 0.772, p = 0.0032).

Results Description

A. Primary Results and Rankings

Figure 1 shows the vote-point totals for all 12 blends, split by evaluation batch and color-coded by generating model. Blend C (GPT-5.2-Pro, Resin Bouquet) is the clear leader with 62 combined points, while Blends F and L form a much smaller second tier at 24 and 23 points. Table 1 provides the exact batch-wise totals for every blend.

Figure 2 aggregates those same vote points at the model level. GPT-5.2-Pro finishes first with 83 points, or about 45% of all points awarded across the two batches. Opus 4.5 follows with 43 points, GPT-5.2-ExtThink with 32, and Gemini 3 Pro with 26.

Figures 3 and 4 repeat the ranking exercise using weighted performance score (WPS), the composite index built from pleasantness, harmony, distinctiveness, strength, and off-notes. Blend C again ranks first among individual blends, and GPT-5.2-Pro again ranks first among models with a mean WPS of 4.10 and a 95% confidence interval of plus or minus 0.28. Tables 2 and 3 report the underlying descriptive statistics.

Figure 5 and Figure 34 compare the two ranking methods directly. The relationship is strongly positive (rho = 0.837, p = 0.001), showing that the vote-point and Likert-based analyses converge on the same overall hierarchy. Figure 31 condenses the model-level result in a single summary image, while Figures 32 and 33 add wearability and least-preferred context to the same ranking story.

B. Statistical Tests

Table 4 reports the Kruskal-Wallis results across models for each sensory attribute and for WPS. The overall WPS comparison is not significant (H = 4.95, p = 0.175, eta squared = 0.009), but two attribute-level comparisons do reach significance: T15 strength (H = 8.09, p = 0.044) and distinctiveness (H = 13.96, p = 0.003).

Table 5 presents Dunn post-hoc pairwise comparisons with Bonferroni correction. No WPS pair remains significant after correction, which is consistent with the modest omnibus effect size and the overlap visible in Figures 15 through 17.

Table 6 reports the Friedman repeated-measures test on the 13 complete cases. The result is not significant (chi squared = 0.93, p = 0.818, Kendall's W = 0.024), indicating that the within-subject ordering is weak in the fully complete subset.

C. Sensory Profiles

Figure 6 compares the top three blends using radar charts. Blend C spans the largest area overall and is highest on T0 pleasantness, T15 pleasantness, and harmony. Figure 7 then averages the five scored dimensions at the model level, where GPT-5.2-Pro again extends furthest on the pleasantness and harmony axes.

Figures 8 and 9 convert the same information into heatmaps. The blend-level heatmap shows Blend C as the darkest combination of pleasantness and harmony values in the dataset, while the model-level heatmap shows GPT-5.2-Pro as the darkest overall model average.

Table 7 contains the descriptive values behind these visuals. Blend C records the highest T0 pleasantness mean (5.29), the highest T15 pleasantness mean (5.11), and the highest harmony mean (5.19) among the blends with complete coverage.

D. Temporal Analysis

Figures 10 and 11 compare T0 and T15 ratings for pleasantness and strength at the blend level. The paired-line format shows that some blends improve, some decline, and others remain relatively stable over the 15-minute blotter interval.

Figures 12 and 13 group those T15 minus T0 changes by model. The strength-change panel aligns with the significant T15 strength result in Table 4, while the pleasantness-change panel shows substantial overlap across models.

Figures 14 and 15 focus specifically on T15 pleasantness distributions. The blend-level view keeps Blend C near the top of the set after development, while the model-level view shows overlap broad enough to match the non-significant omnibus WPS result.

Figure 44 extends the temporal analysis into chemistry space by plotting base-note percentage against pleasantness change from T0 to T15. The near-flat pattern (rho = -0.087, p = 0.798) shows that note architecture did not predict change in pleasantness over this short blotter interval.

E. Attribute Deep-Dives

Figure 16 summarizes harmony scores by model, and Figure 17 does the same for distinctiveness. Harmony shows broad overlap, but distinctiveness separates the models more clearly and corresponds to the strongest attribute-level statistical result in Table 4.

Figures 18 and 19 quantify off-note detection by blend and by model. These charts identify which formulas most often produced medicinal, sharp, or otherwise undesirable side impressions during evaluation.

F. Demographics

Figure 20 summarizes the composition of the 31-person panel, while Figures 21 and 22 break preference down by gender and age group. These subgroup plots are descriptive because several cells are small, but they provide context for whether the overall ranking pattern is stable across participant subsets.

Figures 23 and 24 shift from preference to perceived scent gender. Each blend and each model is classified across masculine, feminine, and unisex impressions, providing another descriptive lens on how the fragrances were perceived.

G. Multivariate and Qualitative Analyses

Figure 25 places the blends in a two-dimensional PCA space, and Figure 26 groups them by hierarchical clustering. Together, these figures show that the high-performing sophisticated blends occupy a distinct region of sensory space relative to the more citrus-medicinal formulas.

Figure 27 combines positive vote points and negative Bottom-1 counts into a single polarization view. Blend C stands apart as the clearest consensus winner because it combines the highest positive total with relatively little rejection.

Figures 28 through 30 summarize the qualitative comments collected from participants. The combined and model-specific word clouds highlight recurring descriptors such as floral, rose, woody, sweet, candle, and bubblegum, while the frequency bar chart counts the most common terms directly.

H. Chemistry Analysis

Figure 35 and Table 8 describe note architecture for the 12 blends. Blend C contains 56.7% base notes compared with roughly 22% across the full set, and Figure 36 shows that GPT-5.2-Pro has the highest average base-note percentage at the model level.

Figure 37 maps fragrance-family composition, and Figure 38 plus Table 9 break each blend into terpene and related classes. The highest-ranked blends cluster around floral, resinous, and woody families and show more heavy-terpene content than the lower-ranked citrus-medicinal blends.

Figure 39 and Table 11 compare functional-group diversity with vote points. The relationship is positive but not significant. Figure 43 and Table 11 show a stronger positive association for Shannon molecular diversity (rho = 0.674, p = 0.0163).

Figure 40 and Figure 41 address creative distance from the reference dataset. The scatter in Figure 40 shows a positive relationship between NOT-IN-DATASET percentage and vote points (rho = 0.645, p = 0.0235), while Figure 41 shows that GPT-5.2-Pro has the highest average creativity score.

Figure 42 shows pairwise chemical overlap among all 12 blends. The strongest similarity is between C and L (Jaccard = 0.93), even though their vote-point totals differ substantially. Figure 45 identifies which compounds recur most often across the blend set, and Figure 46 summarizes each LLM's chemistry strategy on five axes in one radar plot.

Table 10 records which oils appear in which formulas, and Table 11 gathers the chemistry-preference correlations in one place. The strongest observed association is between vote points and base-note percentage (rho = 0.772, p = 0.0032).

Figures and Tables

All available analysis visuals and tables are embedded below in the same page flow for Google Sites-friendly use. The linked version keeps file size manageable for local editing, and the portable version in the same folder inlines the images for upload-based use.

Discussion

Which model performed best?

GPT-5.2-Pro is the strongest overall performer by both ranking systems used in the project. It wins the vote-point comparison by a wide margin and also leads the model-level WPS comparison. Because the two metrics agree strongly, the result does not depend on a single scoring method.

At the same time, the model-level win is concentrated in one outstanding formula. Blend C contributes 62 of GPT-5.2-Pro's 83 vote points, so the model's advantage is best described as hit-driven rather than uniformly dominant across all three of its blends.

How should the significance tests be interpreted?

The omnibus WPS comparison does not reach significance, and that matters. The ranking pattern is clear, but the full distributions still overlap enough that the study cannot claim a large average performance gap across all models under all conditions.

That combination of a clear winner and a non-significant omnibus average is not contradictory. It suggests that the strongest difference appears in the best outputs rather than in every output, which fits a creative task such as fragrance formulation where one exceptional blend can outweigh two merely adequate ones.

Why did the sophisticated blends do best?

The three highest vote-point blends - C, F, and L - were all generated for the Sophisticated target profile. One possibility is that the profile itself encourages note pyramids with more depth, more supporting base materials, and more sensory complexity than the Accessible or Distinctive prompts.

A second possibility is that all four LLMs invested their best formulation logic into the Sophisticated assignment because it sounds the most perfumery-specific and therefore invites richer chemical choices. The two explanations are compatible, and both point to the same practical observation: the strongest outputs came from formulas designed for depth rather than freshness or novelty alone.

How does chemistry explain the sensory ranking?

The chemistry results are the strongest part of the project because they connect human preference back to measurable formulation properties. Four chemistry metrics show positive and significant relationships with vote points: base-note percentage, Shannon diversity, NOT-IN-DATASET percentage, and sesquiterpene-plus-diterpene percentage.

Base-note percentage is the strongest predictor. In perfumery terms, heavier molecules tend to evaporate more slowly and support the overall scent structure. Blend C's 56.7% base-note architecture, combined with frankincense, sandalwood, vanilla, rose, and jasmine, gave it a slower and more layered scent profile than the lighter, more monoterpene-heavy formulas.

Creativity relative to the reference dataset also matters. Models that stayed close to the EOCalc examples produced some of the weakest blends, while the highest-rated formulas introduced oils that were largely absent from that reference set. That suggests the best-performing system did not merely copy known combinations; it recombined the palette into a chemically richer space.

What does the C versus L comparison show?

Figure 42 creates a useful contrast between similarity and preference. Blends C and L share an extremely high predicted compound overlap, yet C wins 62 vote points and L wins 23. The difference implies that fragrance performance depends on more than broad ingredient overlap alone.

Small formulation details matter: drop ratios, the balance between bright and warm materials, and the presence or absence of a few bridge ingredients can shift how a scent opens, settles, and is judged by people. That is an important chemistry lesson because it shows why percentage-level formulation design can matter even when the ingredient list looks almost the same.

Blend L also has limited Likert coverage because it was added later to the evaluation workflow. That limitation means the project should treat L cautiously in any comparison that depends on the full multi-attribute score set.

How should the null temporal result be read?

Figure 44 is valuable precisely because it is not significant. Base notes clearly predict absolute preference across the set, but they do not predict short-term change in pleasantness from T0 to T15 on blotter strips.

The most likely explanation is the test medium. Blotter strips at room temperature do not reproduce skin temperature, oil absorption, or body-chemistry interactions, so a 15-minute blotter window may compress the real-world differences in evaporation behavior. In other words, note architecture matters, but this specific short blotter test is not sensitive enough to show that effect as a pleasantness-change slope.

How does this fit the current literature?

The project fits well with recent work on AI-assisted formulation and computational odor representation. Studies by Di Guardo et al., Aleixandre et al., Sisson et al., Kurfali et al., and Sharma et al. all point toward AI systems being able to navigate fragrance or odor space in useful ways, but they also emphasize that olfaction remains a hard domain.

This study adds a human-evaluation bridge to that literature. Instead of only predicting odor attributes from molecules, it compares generative model outputs in a blind sensory setting and then ties the winning formulas back to volatility structure, molecular diversity, and creativity in chemical space.

Limitations

The panel size is adequate for a student study but still modest for detecting small model-level effects. The sample is also a convenience sample rather than a trained perfumer panel, so the results should be read as human consumer-style judgments rather than professional fragrance grading.

The chemistry metrics are estimated from literature-derived component profiles rather than direct GC-MS analysis of the exact oil bottles used. That makes the chemistry comparisons useful and internally consistent, but still approximate.

The largest design limitation is incomplete Likert coverage for Blend L and the rest of the GPT-5.2-ExtThink set. The late addition of that blend narrows what can be concluded from the full multi-attribute comparison.

Conclusion

Conclusion

The hypothesis was supported. GPT-5.2-Pro produced the most preferred set of essential-oil perfume blends under the conditions of this project, and its Blend C was the clearest overall winner in blind evaluation.

The strongest result is not only that one LLM won, but that the ranking can be explained chemically. The best-performing blends had more base-note structure, more molecular diversity, greater use of oils outside the reference dataset, and more heavy terpene content than the weakest blends.

The study also shows that near-matching ingredient space does not guarantee near-matching human response. Blend C and Blend L are chemically very similar on paper, yet participants responded much more positively to C, which highlights how sensitive fragrance performance is to exact compositional balance.

Most important findings

  • GPT-5.2-Pro ranked first by both vote points and weighted performance score.
  • Blend C was the standout blend with 62 vote points and the highest pleasantness and harmony scores in the set.
  • Vote-point rankings and WPS rankings converged strongly (rho = 0.837, p = 0.001).
  • Base-note percentage was the strongest chemistry predictor of preference (rho = 0.772, p = 0.0032).
  • Molecular diversity and NOT-IN-DATASET creativity also correlated positively with preference.
  • Distinctiveness and T15 strength were the clearest attribute-level differences between models.
  • Base-note percentage did not predict short-term pleasantness change on blotter strips, showing that absolute preference and temporal change are not the same outcome.

Significance and future work

The main significance is that the sensory outcome is mechanistically interpretable. The winning formulas were not just preferred; they were measurably richer in note architecture and chemical diversity.

Future work should expand the panel size, repeat the test on skin rather than only on blotter strips, and validate the blend chemistry with GC-MS on the actual oils used. It would also be useful to compare frontier LLMs against fragrance-specific generative models using the same evaluation protocol.
